chrome.utility: Open protocol · v1.0 public preview · Apache-2.0

reg.title: When AI decisions matter, the record has to stand on its own.
reg.sub: AI governance is moving from policy statements to evidence. TSP gives teams a standard way to preserve what happened, bind it cryptographically, and make it independently checkable later.
reg.framing: For teams working with the EU AI Act, DORA, internal controls, procurement reviews, and incident response, the question is no longer only whether a system performs. It is whether the record can be examined, challenged, retained, and trusted after the fact.
reg.future: The durable advantage is not only capability. It is provability.
reg.cta: See the full regulatory stress test →

hero.sub: Trust Standard Protocol gives AI systems a standard way to produce tamper-evident, portable evidence of what happened — so teams, auditors, customers, and regulators can verify the record without trusting the vendor.
hero.rcpt_aria: A live decision receipt. It verifies with real Ed25519 in your browser; break one byte and the seal fails.
hero.verdict_invalid: Failed — one byte changed, the seal broke

problem.title: AI decisions are becoming easier to produce — and harder to prove.
problem.prob_title: Most evidence stays inside vendor-controlled systems.
problem.prob_body: Logs, dashboards, and internal audit trails may be useful operationally, but they are difficult for an outside party to verify independently once a dispute, review, or regulatory question appears.
prob_points: Records can be changed, filtered, or reconstructed after the fact.
Review depends on internal access, screenshots, or vendor explanation.
Counterparties cannot easily reproduce the same integrity result themselves.
problem.sol_title: Runtime events become signed, portable evidence.
problem.sol_body: TSP turns AI-mediated events into tamper-evident receipts that can be checked independently and offline — giving teams a portable evidence layer for auditability, oversight, investigations, and trust.
sol_points: Tamper-evident: one changed byte breaks the verification result.
Portable: the evidence can travel beyond the issuing system.
Independently checkable: anyone with the receipt and key can re-run the math.

promise: If an AI system made or supported a decision, the record should be independently verifiable.

how.title: Three steps. Clear boundary. Repeatable verification.

steps.title: The event is sealed
steps.body: A TrustEnvelope records the event in canonical form and seals it cryptographically so later changes are detectable.

steps.title: The record can be anchored
steps.body: Receipts can be chained and, where needed, independently anchored to strengthen evidentiary posture beyond a self-attested local record.

steps.title: Anyone verifies
steps.body: The cryptography can be re-run offline, producing the same integrity result for every verifier.

items.title: Tamper-evident
items.body: If the signed record changes, verification fails. Quiet edits do not survive inspection.

items.title: Portable
items.body: Evidence can move across teams, customers, auditors, and regulators without depending on the issuing vendor's dashboard.

items.title: Independently checkable
items.body: Verification does not require trust in us or in the issuer — only the published rules, keys, and cryptography.

whytsp.title: Four properties that make evidence useful under scrutiny.

items.title: Signed receipts
items.body: Each event is sealed as a TrustEnvelope — canonical JSON, hashed with SHA-256, signed at the point of issuance.

items.title: Portable verification
items.body: Anyone can re-run the Ed25519 verification offline. The evidence is not trapped behind an API, account, or vendor workflow.

items.title: Reviewable records
items.body: The trail stays legible to the people who must assess it — engineering, compliance, audit, procurement, and oversight.

items.title: Governed status
items.body: Official status is governed separately from technical verification. Payment never grants it, and verification alone does not confer it.

futures.title: Three futures for AI evidence.
futures.sub: Every AI deployment is moving toward one of these models. Only one produces portable, independently verifiable records.

items.label: Future 01
items.title: No proof
items.body: Outputs matter, but the evidence trail is incomplete, fragile, or absent.

items.label: Future 02
items.title: Private proof
items.body: Each organization builds its own evidence workflow, but verification remains local and non-portable.

items.label: Future 03 · With TSP
items.title: Standardized proof
items.body: Outputs carry portable, cryptographically verifiable evidence that different parties can inspect independently.

boundary.title: What verification means — and what it does not.
boundary.after: . It does not prove an output was correct, lawful, safe, or compliant. Verification reports the integrity of the record — not the legal meaning of the outcome.

prov.lead: Soon, AI vendors will not compete only on capability.
prov.honest: TSP does not replace legal, governance, or compliance programs. It gives those functions something they often lack: a portable technical evidence layer at the point where outputs are created and reviewed.
prov.axiom: And one boundary keeps the system defensible: verification is not truth. A receipt can prove what a system recorded, signed, and declared at a moment in time — not whether the underlying decision was substantively correct or legally justified.
prov.proof_line: TSP turns AI outputs into verifiable evidence.

scope.title: What TSP covers — and what it doesn't.
scope.sub: Useful systems define their boundary precisely. TSP covers the technical evidence layer; it does not claim to perform the legal, organisational, or adjudicative parts around it.
covers: Tamper-evident records of outputs, declared sources, and process context.
Ed25519 signatures resolving to a published manifest — including key state and verification context.
Independent, offline verification with shared rules and conformance fixtures.
Technical support for logging, oversight, disclosure, audit, and retention workflows.
doesnt: Make a system legally compliant by itself.
Determine whether an output was correct, fair, or lawful.
Replace conformity assessment, DPIA, FRIA, or governance programs.
Grant official status through payment or self-declaration.

proof.title: Watch a receipt break in real time.
proof.body: This verifier runs entirely in your browser against the issuer's Ed25519 public key. Nothing is uploaded. Change one byte and the verification result fails.
proof.caption: Real Ed25519 + SHA-256 · offline verification · no hidden trust in the issuer

whouses.title: For builders, reviewers, and counterparties who need the record to travel.

items.body: Add a verifiable evidence layer to AI workflows without making verification depend on your own platform.
Work from evidence that can be retained, reviewed, and challenged with a clear technical boundary.
Inspect a decision trail independently, without relying on screenshots, vendor claims, or internal dashboard access.
Exchange AI records across organizational boundaries with a shared verification method.

final.title: Build for the review that happens after deployment.
final.sub: Make AI evidence verifiable before a regulator, customer, auditor, or counterparty asks for it. The reference verifier is open and runs offline.

footer.blurb: An open protocol for verifiable AI evidence — durable, tamper-evident, and independently checkable. The integrity of the record, not a judgment of the outcome.
footer.legal: Open protocol · v1.0 public preview · Apache-2.0
footer.disclaimer: TSP verifies evidence integrity within an instrumented boundary. It does not determine factual correctness, legality, safety, or compliance.
